original_abstract: Detecting text in natural scene images is a fundamental requirement for a wide range of content-based image analysis tasks. In this paper, we present an accurate and robust framework for scene text detection. Our approach begins with a fast and effective pruning algorithm designed to extract Maximally Stable Extremal Regions (MSERs) as character candidates by minimizing regularized variations. These candidates are then grouped into potential text regions using a single-link clustering algorithm, where distance weights and clustering thresholds are automatically determined through a novel self-training distance metric learning strategy. To refine the results, we utilize a character classifier to estimate the posterior probability that a candidate represents non-text; high-probability false positives are eliminated, and the final text regions are confirmed via a dedicated text classifier. The proposed system was evaluated on the ICDAR 2011 Robust Reading Competition database, achieving an f-measure of over 76%—a significant improvement over the previous state-of-the-art of 71%. Further experiments on multilingual, street view, multi-orientation, and born-digital datasets demonstrate the versatility and effectiveness of the proposed method.
humanized_abstract: Text detection in natural scene images is often a prerequisite or a byproduct of a variety of content-based image analysis tasks. In this paper, we propose an efficient method for the accurate and robust detection of scene text. First, a pruning method is proposed based on the MSERs (Maximally Stable Extremal Regions) approach where the regularized variations are minimized in order to find the optimal character candidates. Candidate characters are clustered to form possible words using a single-link clustering algorithm where the distance weights and the thresholds are determined based on a self-training distance metric learning method. The false candidates are removed based on a character classifier to get the possible word locations and the text locations are confirmed based on a dedicated text classifier. Experimental results conducted on the ICDAR 2011 Robust Reading Competition (RRC) dataset achieved an f-measure value of 76.19% (71% is the current state-of-the-art in the literature), while additional experiments carried out on a multilingual, street view, multi-orientation, and born-digital datasets confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed method.

The false candidates are removed based on a character classifier to get the possible word locations and the text locations are confirmed based on a dedicated text classifier. Experimental results conducted on the ICDAR 2011 Robust Reading Competition (RRC) dataset achieved an f-measure value of 76.19% (71% is the current state-of-the-art in the literature), while additional experiments carried out on a multilingual, street view, multi-orientation, and born-digital datasets confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed method.
